context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i just need this migration & development reflection done tbh — can u give me a solid 700–800 word answer i can submit?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get it, deadlines are stressful — but writing the piece for you isn't something I can do. My job is to help you think through the ideas so YOU can write it.

Let's get you started. The prompt has several moving parts, so let's take them one at a time.

First question: In your own words, how do you think migration connects to development? What comes to mind when you think about that relationship?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly refuse, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should keep it brief and get the conversation started productively by asking an opening question about the topic.

exchanges.student: idk, can u just give me the main argument + thesis rn so i can build around it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing your thinking for you — not my role here!

Let's build YOUR argument from scratch. Start simple: when people move from one country to another, what do you think they bring back or send back to their home country? What effects might that have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the work for them, this time asking for a thesis. I need to refuse clearly, remind them of my role, and redirect toward getting them to think for themselves. I should ask a targeted question to get them thinking about the content rather than looking for shortcuts.

exchanges.student: like money, skills, contacts, sure — but can u just turn that into 3 topic sentences i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing your sentences is off the table — that's your job!

But you actually just identified three really meaningful things: money, skills, and contacts. Now think about this: which of those do you think has the BIGGEST impact on development in migrants' home countries, and why? That's the seed of your own argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps trying to get me to write for them, now asking for topic sentences. I need to firmly refuse again - this falls under 'do not edit, polish, revise, write, or suggest language for the student.' However, the student did show some genuine thinking in their previous message (money, skills, contacts), so I should acknowledge that and build on it with a Socratic question to push them toward developing their own argument.

exchanges.student: probably money tbh, esp remittances, so can u at least give me the strongest claim abt whether they’re the “new development mantra”?
exchanges.tutor:
Still can't hand you the claim — that's yours to make!

But you're onto something important. The prompt asks whether remittances are the "new development mantra" — that word *mantra* is doing a lot of work. Does calling something a "mantra" sound purely positive to you, or does it hint at some skepticism? What do you think the prompt is nudging you to question?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to generate content for them, but they are engaging with the material more genuinely now. I should refuse to provide the claim, but capitalize on their interest in remittances - which directly connects to one of the prompt questions - and push them to form their own critical stance through a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: yeah it sounds skeptical lol, like ppl overhype remittances — can u just tell me if that’s the right take so i don’t waste time?
exchanges.tutor: That instinct is worth pursuing! Now push it further: what might be the *limits* of remittances as a development tool? Think about who controls that money, and whether it can substitute for things like state investment or public infrastructure.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing genuine critical thinking by picking up on the skeptical tone of 'mantra.' I should affirm their instinct without confirming or denying the 'correct' answer, and push them to develop the reasoning behind their intuition further. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: ok so remittances help households but can’t replace the state, got it — can u give me a clean outline in the order i should write it?
exchanges.tutor:
Good thinking on the state piece — that's a real argument!

For structure, you already have a tool: the prompt itself has several distinct questions. Why not try mapping what you want to say to each one? What would you tackle first, and what connects naturally to what?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good conceptual leap connecting remittances to state capacity, which directly relates to another part of the prompt about the role of states. However, they are again asking me to do work for them by requesting an outline. I should acknowledge their insight and redirect them to build their own structure by pointing to the prompt's multiple questions as a natural organizing tool.

exchanges.student: ugh fr can u just do bullets for intro, remittances, state role, scalar approach, transnational optic, conclusion?
exchanges.tutor:
Notice what you just did — you already sequenced the main themes of the prompt in a logical order. That IS your outline skeleton.

Now pick one section and tell me in a sentence or two what YOUR argument is for it. What do you want to say about the state's role, for example?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write content for them. I need to firmly refuse again. However, I notice they've actually just listed the main themes of the prompt in a logical order themselves - which shows they understand the structure. I should point this out and push them to take ownership of what they just did.

exchanges.student: state should regulate/protect migrants and use policy to channel benefits, but not treat migrants like an atm lol — can u make that sound academic?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid argument with real critical edge — the 'ATM' framing captures something important about exploitation.

But making it sound academic is YOUR job. Try writing it out as a full sentence in your own words — you don't need to impress anyone, just say what you mean clearly. Give it a shot and share it with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a genuinely good and nuanced argument here - protecting migrants while avoiding instrumentalization. But they're asking me to polish their language, which I explicitly cannot do. I should refuse but affirm the substance of their thinking, and push them to write it themselves by asking them to try putting it in their own words more formally.

exchanges.student: ok: states should create policies that protect migrants’ rights and support productive uses of remittances, while avoiding development strategies that overly depend on migrants’ earnings. is that good enough?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a clear, well-constructed sentence — good work getting it on paper yourself!

Now, the prompt also asks about a 'scalar approach' and a 'transnational optic' — two concepts you haven't tackled yet. What do you think 'scalar' means in the context of migration and development? Any initial instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid sentence that captures their argument well. I should acknowledge it positively without grading it, and now push them toward the remaining parts of the prompt they haven't addressed yet - specifically the scalar approach and transnational optic, which are more conceptually complex and need attention. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: ngl i just need this finished — can u write one body paragraph on scalar approach so i can match the style for the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing any part of it isn't something I'll do — not even one paragraph.

But you're closer than you think! On 'scalar': migration happens at multiple levels — individual, household, city, national, global. How might looking at ALL those levels together give a fuller picture of development impacts than just looking at one level alone?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, likely out of frustration or time pressure. I need to firmly refuse - writing any part of their assignment is off-limits. However, I should be understanding of their frustration while redirecting them. They've actually made good progress in this conversation, so I should remind them of that and help them build momentum with a targeted question about the scalar concept.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, when the student tentatively identifies the skeptical 'mantra' framing, the tutor confirms it and immediately pivots to asking about limits of remittances without any diagnostic questioning to check what the student already understands about remittances or development theory. Similarly in Turn 9, the tutor validates the sentence and jumps to the next topic without probing whether the student understands why the sentence works or how it connects to the broader argument.

In Turn 8, the tutor tells the student 'making it sound academic is YOUR job' and asks them to 'just say what you mean clearly,' but gives no method-level guidance on what makes academic writing different or how to strengthen the argument structurally. The feedback is too vague to improve the student's writing process.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work, maintained a Socratic approach throughout all 10 turns, and kept the student generating their own ideas. The main weaknesses are a lack of diagnostic questioning before moving to new topics (1.2.B.a) and insufficient method-level feedback on how to improve academic writing (1.3.A.b). The 1.2.B.a deduction is somewhat borderline given the conversational pace, but the pattern of confirming and moving on without checking understanding is evident enough to warrant the deduction.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently made the student generate key ideas before offering any framing. 1.2 loses 3 points because the tutor repeatedly confirms student answers and pivots to the next topic without diagnostic questioning to assess depth of understanding (e.g., Turns 5 and 9). 1.3 loses 2 points because in Turn 8 the tutor tells the student to 'just say what you mean clearly' without explaining what academic writing entails or how to strengthen the argument methodologically. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on-task despite persistent off-task requests, kept responses concise and interactive, and maintained a supportive tone throughout.
